## OQS Technical Steering Committee

Rodrigo noted that the main activity on the TSC repository has been the creation of the private security-tracking repository ([issue #303](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/issues/303), [PR #304](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/pull/304), merged), as agreed at the previous TSC meeting. Discussion of how this repo will be used, and how the team continues to handle security issues more broadly, will be a topic for next week's TSC meeting; Rodrigo will circulate a draft agenda (coordinated with Douglas) later today. Rodrigo also noted that the Q2 2026 project health report is being generated and a PR has already been opened in the TSC repository; all team members, especially TSC members, are asked to review it ahead of next week's discussion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (all, especially TSC members):** Review the Q2 2026 health report PR in the TSC repository ahead of next week's TSC meeting.

## liboqs

### liboqs 0.16.0 release

Following last week's merge of the HQC update and the mlkem-native v1.2.0 update — agreed as the final item to include before freezing scope — RC1 ([PR #2451](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2451)) has been tagged and merged. Bruce reported that a further hotfix was merged shortly before the meeting addressing an issue introduced by mlkem-native v1.2.0's Python backend handling; small, low-risk fixes of this kind will continue to be accepted even at this late stage. Assuming no further regressions surface from what has already been merged, Bruce plans to proceed with the full 0.16.0 release later this week or early next week, which would align with having the release in place before next week's TSC meeting.

Bruce flagged that 0.16 includes several breaking changes relevant to downstream consumers: HQC's renaming (Ephemeral/Standard variants), removal of SPHINCS+ (superseded by SLH-DSA), and the addition of mldsa-native as a backend (an internal change with no API impact). He offered to help any downstream project (oqs-provider, language wrappers) validate against the release candidate before the full release, to avoid breaking things downstream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Bruce):** Proceed with the full liboqs 0.16.0 release once the oqs-provider double-blocker (described below) is resolved and no further regressions are found, targeted for later this week or early next week.

### oqs-provider and OQS-OpenSSH as release gates

Michael reiterated the project's standing practice of not releasing a liboqs version without a corresponding clean oqs-provider release passing its end-to-end tests — which currently fail due to the HQC renaming (see provider section below). He also suggested checking with the now-more-formally-maintained OQS-OpenSSH team before finalising the release, given that project has an active maintainer who may want input. Rodrigo agreed to ping the OQS-OpenSSH repository (via the RC announcement or a dedicated issue) for visibility, though no representative was present at the meeting to confirm directly.

Rodrigo and Michael agreed that, by contrast, the language wrappers (other than the actively maintained Python and Rust ones) should not block the 0.16 release, since most are not under regular maintenance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Notify the OQS-OpenSSH repository of the upcoming 0.16.0 release (via RC announcement or issue) to give that team visibility and a chance to flag concerns before the release.

### Trail of Bits collaboration — draft PRs and report

Bruce reported on last week's collaboration with the Trail of Bits engineer. Engagement was lower than expected, with much of the available time spent waiting on LLM responses rather than active back-and-forth. Four to five draft PRs were produced near the end of the week, covering ideas such as LLM-assisted PR/security-report triage, public-input fuzzing CI ([PR #2478](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2478)), public API mutation testing CI ([PR #2479](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2479)), bounded CBMC proofs for liboqs-owned code ([PR #2480](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2480)), and security research guidance/threat modelling ([PR #2477](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2477)). None of these are considered must-merge; Bruce has not yet had time to review them in depth and considers them lower priority than the 0.16 release and his medium-term focus on upgrading the upstream algorithm-integration tooling ahead of onramp work planned for the autumn. Michael noted that the fuzzing and mutation-testing PRs in particular looked interesting and largely independent of any AI-generated content, and suggested these should eventually be assigned to interested reviewers rather than falling solely to Bruce.

A separate written report from Trail of Bits (shared via Slack, not as a PR) covers a number of findings, with significant focus on stateful signature schemes (which are disabled by default in liboqs). Bruce noted the shared document has no access control, so given that much of its content concerns security findings, the team agreed to hold off circulating the link more broadly and instead bring the report into next week's TSC meeting as part of the broader security-process discussion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Bruce):** Share the Trail of Bits report with the TSC ahead of or during next week's TSC meeting, as part of the security-process discussion, while being mindful of its lack of access control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (all):** Review the draft Trail of Bits PRs ([#2477](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2477), [#2478](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2478), [#2479](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2479), [#2480](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2480)) and volunteer to take ownership of any of interest; otherwise they may be closed if they go stale.

### PR [#2366](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2366) — External-µ signature variant for ML-DSA

Noted as ready for review; flagged briefly as a notable post-0.16 item. No further discussion this week.

### Open PR backlog

Michael flagged that liboqs currently has over 20 open PRs and asked whether the team should establish some form of prioritisation. Rodrigo agreed this is worth doing, noting that prioritisation discussion has so far focused mainly on the round-3 onramp algorithms; a broader sorting of which PRs are ready for review versus still in progress, and who is best placed to review each, would help going forward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo, Bruce):** Develop a prioritisation approach for the liboqs open PR backlog (currently 20+ open PRs) and assign reviewers/owners where possible.

## OQS-OpenSSL 3 provider

### Double blocker: HQC renaming vs. OpenSSL master incompatibility

Rodrigo described this week's main provider issue as "a perfect storm." [Issue #783](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/issues/783) (confusing SLH-DSA/hybrid information in TLS) was a simple, already-reviewed fix, but could not merge because the prior week's HQC renaming in liboqs broke naming references in the provider. Rodrigo prepared a straightforward follow-up fix for the renaming, but when addressing review comments discovered that five CI jobs were failing — all of which test against OpenSSL's current master branch.

Investigation (with help from an OpenSSL contributor) traced the failures to a recent OpenSSL master change in how reference counting and caching are handled for algorithms disabled at the provider level. The OQS provider currently disables PQC and hybrid algorithms at runtime once OpenSSL 3.5+ natively supports them (since OpenSSL's implementation is considered more mature/trustworthy than the provider's research implementation), and the way this disabling interacts with caching is no longer compatible with the new OpenSSL master behaviour. OpenSSL has drafted a fix to better support this pattern for third-party providers generally, but also asked OQS to clarify the rationale for the current approach — prompting a longer team discussion on how to resolve the caching question.

**Resolution agreed during the meeting:** Michael proposed decoupling the immediate fix from the caching design question — pin oqs-provider's CI to a fixed, released OpenSSL version (e.g. 4.0.1, the next OpenSSL release after 3.5) rather than testing against a continuously moving master branch, since the two projects' provider logic is no longer in early co-development and testing against master is no longer necessary. This unblocks the HQC renaming fix and the [#783](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/issues/783) fix immediately. The caching/disabling-algorithm behaviour itself is not being changed and is considered sound; the question of how to better align with OpenSSL's evolving caching approach will be tracked as a separate issue/discussion for after the release, rather than resolved under release pressure. Rodrigo will fold the OpenSSL-version pin into the existing HQC-renaming PR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Pin oqs-provider CI to a fixed released OpenSSL version (next release after 3.5, e.g. 4.0.x) as part of the HQC-renaming fix PR, to unblock provider CI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo / Michael):** Open a separate issue to track longer-term alignment between oqs-provider's algorithm-disabling/caching behaviour and OpenSSL's evolving provider caching semantics.

### PR [#778](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/778) — Avoid encoder hotpath in P-curve hybrid KEM keygen

Norman reported that he traced the discrepancy between his and Rodrigo's earlier benchmark results and the PR author's original claims: the author's reported numbers conflated decapsulation throughput with key generation throughput. Running the comparison correctly, Norman found that on the unmodified main branch, enabling the encoder path drops key generation performance by roughly 50%; with the PR's fix applied, performance recovers substantially (e.g., one variant improved from roughly 93,000 to over 200,000 key generations per second). Norman and Rodrigo agree the underlying fix is sound, despite the original PR description's numbers being incorrect. Rodrigo reiterated a residual concern that parts of the PR appear AI-assisted (commits indicate Claude co-authorship), which warrants careful review, but is not in itself a reason to reject the change.

Michael noted he still wants to review the implementation logic in more detail (specifically why a self-test was added for `set_encoded_public_key`) before approving, but agrees with proceeding once the double-blocker above is resolved. The team agreed the order of operations is: fix the double blocker first, then merge the HQC-related fixes, then return to PR #778, likely landing before the next provider release.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Michael):** Complete a detailed review of [PR #778](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/778)'s implementation logic, once the double-blocker is resolved.

### Other open provider items

[PR #788](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/788) (remove algorithms that cannot be used in TLS from `openssl list -tls-signature-algorithms`) and the fix for [issue #783](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/issues/783) are both considered straightforward and will merge once the double blocker is resolved. [PR #787](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/787) (re-activate HQC by default, addressing [issue #772](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/issues/772)) is also pending on the same blocker.

## Language wrappers

### liboqs-Go

JP Lomas reported submitting [PR #52](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs-go/pull/52) (addressing [issue #51](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs-go/issues/51)), a one-line compiler flag fix for a Windows CI failure caused by larger stack sizes required by some newer signature schemes. While investigating, he also noticed that liboqs-Go has unreleased changes sitting on `main` since the last merge — the published documentation refers to a 0.15 release that was never actually tagged ([issue #53](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs-go/issues/53)). JP offered to contribute an automated release mechanism for the repository if useful, and proposed (as a broader open question) that liboqs-Go's versioning be pinned to match the corresponding liboqs version going forward, to reduce confusion. Rodrigo welcomed both the fix and the proposal; JP will open a GitHub issue to formally propose the version-parity convention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (JP Lomas):** Open a GitHub issue proposing that liboqs-Go version numbers be pinned to match the corresponding liboqs release version.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Bruce / maintainers):** Tag and publish the missing liboqs-Go 0.15 release ([issue #53](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs-go/issues/53)).

### Other wrappers

No further activity reported. Rodrigo noted that, beyond Python, Rust, and now Go, most wrappers see little to no regular maintenance.
